Notice Of Motion And Motion To Vacate Order Staying Administrative Proceedings And To Reject Jurisdiction (Part 1 Of 2 For Purposes Of Entry Of Tentative Ruling Only
Off Calendar Jun-22-2015 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Alison Berry Wilkinson, of Berry Wilkinson Law Group, Tel. 415.259.6638, for plaintiffs/petitioners;
Adrian G. Driscoll, of Murphy Pearson Bradley & Feeney, Tel. 415.788.1900, for plaintiffs/petitioners;
Kenneth M. Walczak, of Office of the City Attorney, Tel. 415.554.3800, for respondents/defendants;
Scott Burrell, of Burrell Law Office, Tel. 415.806.0087, for Joined party, petitioner.

Ruling: A R G U E D. The Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO, SAN FRANCISCO POLICE DEPARTMENT, SAN FRANCISCO POLICE COMMISSION'S Motion To Vacate Order Staying Administrative Proceedings And To Reject Jurisdiction is denied. The Peace Officers' Procedural Bill of Rights ("POBR"), codified at Gov. Code secs. 3300 et seq. provides due process protections for peace officers. Gov. Code sec. 3309.5(c) provides that the "superior court shall have initial jurisdiction over any proceeding brought by any public safety officer against any public safety department for alleged violations of this chapter." Section 3309.5(d)(1) also provides that if the superior court determines that a public safety department has violated any of the provisions of this chapter, "the court shall render appropriate injunctive relief or other extraordinary relief to remedy the violation'including, but not limited to, the granting of a temporary restraining order, preliminary injunction, or permanent injunction prohibiting the public safety department from taking any punitive action against the public safety officer." Petitioners' ex parte application sets forth sufficient evidence establishing that the City is in violation of Gov. Code sec. 3304(d)(1), which provides that "no punitive action'shall be undertaken for any act, omission, or other allegation of misconduct if the investigation of the allegation is not completed within one year of the public agency's discovery by a person authorized to initiate an investigation of the allegation of an act, omission, or other misconduct." The evidence shows that the City has undertaken punitive action against Petitioners, as defined in Gov. Code sec. 3303. (Declaration of Alison Berry Wilkinson, Ex. D; Declaration of Rain Daugherty, par. 7.) Additionally, Petitioners submit evidence showing that the City is acting beyond the one-year statute of limitations. (Wilkinson Decl., pars. 14, 29, Ex. F.) In its opposition to the ex parte application, the City argues that the Police Chief did not learn about the text messages until January 2015. However, the evidence submitted is insufficient to establish this fact, as Ashley Worsham, an attorney in the Internal Affairs Division of the SFPD, states that she is "informed and believed that Police Chief Sur did not know about the text messages until January 2015." (Declaration of Ashley Worsham, par. 13.) This statement lacks foundation and is based on hearsay. The City also argues that the criminal investigation exception set forth at Gov. Code 3304(d)(2)(A) applies. Again, however, insufficient evidence is set forth to substantiate this argument. The Declaration of Ashley Worsham does not establish that the text messages at issue in this case were the subject of the criminal investigation against Officer Furminger. Thus, the City has not met its burden of showing that the statute of limitations does not bar the discipline. (Continued to Part 2)
